quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "CI for β₁"?

options.text: One-sample t-test applied to the differences: t = d̄/(sd/√n) with df = n-1, testing H₀: μd = 0
b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n - 2; a range of plausible values for the true population slope
The initial probability of an event before new evidence is considered; the starting point in Bayesian reasoning
SD(X ± Y) = √(Var(X) + Var(Y)) for independent random variables; note that SDs do NOT simply add

quiz_questions.explanation: "CI for β₁" is defined as: b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n - 2; a range of plausible values for the true population slope. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Interpretation of slope CI"?

options.text: We are C% confident that for each one-unit increase in x, the mean response y changes by between [lower, upper] units
After forming blocks, using a chance mechanism to assign treatments within each block to balance remaining confounders
Two events A and B are independent if P(A|B) = P(A), meaning knowing B occurred does not change the probability of A
A variable not included in the study that may influence the relationship between the variables being studied

quiz_questions.explanation: "Interpretation of slope CI" is defined as: We are C% confident that for each one-unit increase in x, the mean response y changes by between [lower, upper] units. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Degrees of freedom (regression)"?

options.text: The sampling distribution of p̂ is approximately normal when np ≥ 10 AND n(1-p) ≥ 10 (Large Counts condition)
For Y = a + bX: E(Y) = a + b·E(X) and Var(Y) = b²·Var(X); adding a constant shifts the mean but does not change spread
df = n - 2 for simple linear regression; accounts for estimating both the intercept and slope
The magnitude of the difference between the true parameter and the null value; determines the practical importance of a statistically significant result

quiz_questions.explanation: "Degrees of freedom (regression)" is defined as: df = n - 2 for simple linear regression; accounts for estimating both the intercept and slope. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Zero in the interval"?

options.text: A phenomenon where an association between two variables reverses direction when data are combined across a lurking variable
If 0 is in the CI for β₁, we cannot conclude that x and y have a significant linear relationship at that confidence level
Independence: one random sample classified by two variables; Homogeneity: separate random samples from each population
χ² = Σ(O - E)²/E; sums the squared standardized differences between observed (O) and expected (E) counts across all categories

quiz_questions.explanation: "Zero in the interval" is defined as: If 0 is in the CI for β₁, we cannot conclude that x and y have a significant linear relationship at that confidence level. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reading regression output"?

options.text: Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)
Standard computer output provides coefficients (b₀, b₁), standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and R² for interpretation
(O - E)²/E for each cell; identifies which observed-expected discrepancies drive the overall test result
A measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables; ranges from -1 to +1

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reading regression output" is defined as: Standard computer output provides coefficients (b₀, b₁), standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and R² for interpretation. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n - 2; a range of plausible values for the true population slope"?

options.text: Mean (x̄)
Random assignment
CI for β₁
Mutually exclusive events

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "CI for β₁".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "We are C% confident that for each one-unit increase in x, the mean response y changes by between [lower, upper] units"?

options.text: Statistical significance
Interpretation of slope CI
Residual pattern
False positive/negative

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Interpretation of slope CI".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "df = n - 2 for simple linear regression; accounts for estimating both the intercept and slope"?

options.text: Response bias
Large Counts condition (two samples)
Independent events
Degrees of freedom (regression)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Degrees of freedom (regression)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If 0 is in the CI for β₁, we cannot conclude that x and y have a significant linear relationship at that confidence level"?

options.text: Simpson's Paradox
Heavy tails
Margin of error
Zero in the interval

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Zero in the interval".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Standard computer output provides coefficients (b₀, b₁), standard errors, t-statistics, p-values, and R² for interpretation"?

options.text: Reading regression output
Standard deviation of a sum/difference
Pooled proportion
Systematic sample

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Reading regression output".

options.text: The initial probability of an event before new evidence is considered; the starting point in Bayesian reasoning

(O - E)²/E for each cell; identifies which observed-expected discrepancies drive the overall test result

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Confidence Intervals for Slopes is accurate?

options.text: The geometric distribution counts the number of trials until the first success: P(X=k) = (1-p)^(k-1)·p, with mean 1/p.
Confidence Intervals for Slopes estimate the true population slope β₁ using b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n-2.

Central Limit Theorem (CLT) states that regardless of the population's shape, the sampling distribution of x̄ approaches a normal distribution as sample size n increases (generally n ≥ 30 is sufficient).
Confidence Intervals for Means estimate an unknown population mean μ using x̄ ± t*(s/√n), where t* comes from the t-distribution with df = n-1.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Slopes as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Confidence Intervals for Slopes?

options.text: The interval provides a range of plausible values for the rate of change in y per unit change in x.
Simple random sampling (SRS) gives every possible sample of size n an equal chance of selection, while stratified, cluster, and systematic sampling offer advantages in specific contexts.
This test is equivalent to testing whether the correlation coefficient ρ equals zero.
Measures of center include the mean (arithmetic average sensitive to outliers), the median (resistant middle value), and the mode.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Slopes as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Confidence Intervals for Slopes?

options.text: If the interval does not contain 0, we have evidence that x and y are linearly related.
Discrete Random Variables take countable values (often integers), with each value having an associated probability.
The slope b = r(sy/sx) represents the predicted change in y for a one-unit increase in x, while the y-intercept a gives the predicted y when x = 0.

Probability quantifies uncertainty on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain), with the Law of Large Numbers stating that relative frequency approaches true probability as the number of trials increases.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Slopes as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Confidence Intervals for Slopes?

options.text: The degrees of freedom are approximated by the Welch formula (or conservatively using the smaller of n₁-1 and n₂-1).
Observational data can reveal associations but not causation, because confounding variables may drive the apparent relationship.
Technology output typically provides b₁, SE(b₁), t, and p-value directly, so interpreting computer regression output is an essential skill.
The choice of transformation depends on the curvature pattern, and predictions must be back-transformed to the original scale.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Slopes as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Confidence Intervals for Slopes?

options.text: The three principles of experimental design are control (using comparison groups), randomization (assigning treatments randomly to reduce bias), and replication (using enough subjects to detect real effects).
Understanding this distinction is critical—many important questions (smoking's effect on health, educational interventions) rely on observational data when experiments are unethical or impractical.
Controlling includes keeping conditions identical across groups, standardizing procedures, and using control groups as baselines for comparison.
Confidence Intervals for Slopes estimate the true population slope β₁ using b₁ ± t*·SE(b₁) with df = n-2.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Confidence Intervals for Slopes as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Slopes?

options.text: Epidemiological prospective cohort studies (like the Framingham Heart Study) tracking thousands of people over decades to identify cardiovascular risk factors
Medical research blocking patients by disease severity before randomly assigning drug vs. placebo within each severity block
Astronomy: the power relationship between a planet's orbital period and distance from the sun (Kepler's Third Law: T² ∝ r³) linearized using log-log transformation
A real estate analyst constructing a 95% CI for the slope of price vs. square footage: 'Each additional square foot increases price by $150 to $210'

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Slopes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Confidence Intervals for Slopes?

options.text: Classifying patient data in clinical trials as categorical (treatment group) versus quantitative (blood pressure reading) to select appropriate analysis methods
Medical researchers estimating the rate at which blood pressure changes per year of age, with a confidence interval quantifying the uncertainty in the estimate
Analyzing biological measurements (human heights, birth weights) that naturally approximate normal distributions to identify atypical values
Healthcare studies using cluster sampling of hospitals rather than individual patients when a complete patient list is impractical

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Slopes.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Confidence Intervals for Slopes applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Social media analysis estimating the proportion of posts containing misinformation and computing a confidence interval using the normal approximation to p̂
Reading Minitab/R/Excel regression output to identify the slope estimate, standard error, and confidence interval for a research report
Political polling organizations using stratified sampling by demographic groups (age, region, party) to produce accurate election forecasts
Hospital data examining whether survival rates (survived/died) are conditionally distributed differently across different treatment regimens

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Slopes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Confidence Intervals for Slopes?

options.text: Public health research testing whether smoking status (smoker/non-smoker) is independent of lung disease status (yes/no) in a random sample of adults
A/B testing at tech companies: testing whether a new feature increases click-through rate, with H₀: no difference and p-value determining rollout decisions
A real estate analyst constructing a 95% CI for the slope of price vs. square footage: 'Each additional square foot increases price by $150 to $210'
Medical trials comparing average recovery times between a new surgical technique and the standard procedure across two independent patient groups

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Slopes.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Confidence Intervals for Slopes in practice?

options.text: Tobacco litigation using decades of observational evidence (cohort studies, dose-response relationships) to establish smoking as a cause of lung cancer when experiments were unethical
Climate scientists testing whether year is a significant predictor of global temperature anomaly (testing for a statistically significant warming trend)
Medical researchers estimating the rate at which blood pressure changes per year of age, with a confidence interval quantifying the uncertainty in the estimate
College admissions comparing applicants who took different standardized tests (SAT vs. ACT) by converting to common z-score scale

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Confidence Intervals for Slopes.
